metadata.sources: RSMeans residential 2026, AFA contractor surveys, manufacturer pricing, BBB fencing complaint patterns

materialTiers.label: Chain link 4-foot galvanized
materialTiers.notes: Cheapest. Galvanized steel mesh. Vinyl-coated upgrade available (+25%).

materialTiers.label: Pressure-treated pine (6-foot privacy)
materialTiers.notes: Most common privacy fence. UC4A ground-contact rated posts mandatory.

materialTiers.label: Western red cedar (6-foot privacy)
materialTiers.notes: Naturally rot-resistant. Premium look. Stain every 2-3 years for max life.

materialTiers.label: Redwood (6-foot privacy)
materialTiers.notes: Most rot-resistant wood option. Premium pricing, especially outside CA.

materialTiers.label: Vinyl/PVC privacy
materialTiers.notes: No staining, no maintenance. Brittle in deep cold. Hollow rails can flex in high winds.

materialTiers.label: Aluminum ornamental
materialTiers.notes: Looks like wrought iron without rust. Common for pool fencing (with self-closing gates per code).

materialTiers.label: Composite (Trex Seclusions, etc)
materialTiers.notes: Wood-look without maintenance. Newer category — long-term performance still being established.

materialTiers.label: Horizontal modern cedar / hardwood
materialTiers.notes: Architectural/contemporary look. Higher labor cost due to leveling and post spacing precision.

materialTiers.label: Stone or masonry wall
materialTiers.notes: Highest cost. Permanent. May trigger HOA and zoning review.

wood_priv_150ft.label: 150 LF wood privacy fence (6-ft pressure-treated pine)

pool_fence_150ft.label: 150 LF aluminum pool fence with self-closing gate (code compliant)

supportingComponents.notes: Posts set below frost line: 36-48 in cold climates, 24+ in warm. Skipping this means heave damage in 1 year.
Standard PT pine posts often rated UC3 (above-ground) — wrong for ground contact. Demand UC4A.

supportingComponents.item: Tear-out and disposal of existing fence

redFlagPatterns.explanation: Post depth not specified. Posts must be set below the local frost line — 36-48 inches in cold climates. Shallow posts heave with the first freeze. The single biggest cause of premature fence failure.

Pressure-treated wood posts without UC4A (ground contact) rating specified. Standard PT pine posts are often UC3 (above-ground) — wrong for ground contact. Posts will rot in 5-7 years instead of 20+. Demand UC4A in writing.
No concrete post setting mentioned. Permanent fences need posts set in concrete. Gravel-only is acceptable only in sandy well-drained soils — not the default.

Pool fence with no mention of code compliance. Most jurisdictions require 48-inch minimum, self-closing self-latching gates, and 4-inch maximum vertical gap. Non-compliant pool fencing creates liability and won't pass inspection.
No mention of property line verification or survey. Building over the line creates legal disputes. HOA approval often required even for replacement fences.

redFlagPatterns.regex: \b(811|call before you dig|utility locate|locate request|underground utilities)\b
redFlagPatterns.explanation: No mention of 811/utility locate before digging. Federal law requires it. Hitting a gas or electric line during post digging can be fatal and contractor liable.
No workmanship warranty. Reputable fence companies offer 1-5 year workmanship warranties.
Deposit above 50% on a fence is high risk. Material is the largest cost and is delivered up front, but reputable installers cap deposit at 25-50%.
Vinyl/aluminum/composite fence with no brand named. Quality varies wildly.

scopeCheckItems.label: Post depth below frost line specified

stateLicensing.notes: Fence installers are typically licensed under the state general contractor or home improvement license. Many states exempt small jobs.

questionsToAskContractor: What material and grade are you quoting?
How deep will the posts go? (Below frost line for my area?)
If wood, are the posts UC4A ground-contact rated?
Will posts be set in concrete?
Have you verified my property line and HOA requirements?
Have you scheduled an 811 utility locate?
Is a permit required and who pulls it?
If this is a pool fence, does it meet local code (height, self-closing gate, gap)?
What gate hardware is included — hinges, latch, closer?
